unitTitle: KS2 Y6 Web page creation (Teach Computing — adapted)

lessons.title: What makes a good website?
lessons.objectives:
I can explore a website
I can name the different types of media used on websites
I know that websites are written in HTML
lessons.outline:
Routine first (same every lesson). Words on the board: website · web page · browser · media · HTML. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet open and a device ready; the activity worksheet ready. Read the three 'I can…' aloud, slowly.
1. (0–8) Starter — websites we use. A website is a set of pages; a web page is ONE page; a browser is what we look at them with. Pupils write a website they use and choose what a browser/web page is. Likely mix-up: website vs web page → fix-words 'a web page is ONE page; a website is the WHOLE set.' Keep answers age-appropriate.
2. (8–22) I-do → we-do — quick quest. Show the San Diego Zoo Kids website. Model finding a fact (menu → Animals → Tigers). Pupils find one fact and say HOW they found it (menu, button, search). Support: there is more than one way to find an answer.
3. (22–38) The media on a page. Media = text, pictures, video, sound. Pupils MULTI-SELECT the media they can find on the site. Core: name two types.
4. (38–50) You-do — HTML. Websites are written in HTML (Hypertext Markup Language); the browser READS it to draw the page (it is not run like a program). Likely error: 'the website is programmed' → fix-words 'the browser reads the markup.' Show View page source; pupils view the code and screenshot it.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the three 'I can…'; each pupil says what HTML stands for.
Differentiation on the SAME task, low floor / high ceiling. No flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine. TA: prompt, do not click for them.

lessons.title: Becoming a web designer
lessons.objectives:
I can recognise the common features of a web page
I can suggest media to include on my page
I can draw a web page layout that suits my purpose
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Words on the board: logo · header · layout · media · purpose · audience. TA: the starter and activity worksheets open; the design sheet and help sheet ready; paper and pencils. Read the three 'I can…' aloud. Recap: a website is a set of pages; a web page is one page.
1. (0–8) Starter — a web designer. A web designer plans and builds pages; first they think about PURPOSE (why) and AUDIENCE (who for). Likely mix-up: purpose vs audience → fix-words 'purpose is WHY, audience is WHO.' Pupils choose what 'purpose' means and write the purpose/audience of their own page.
2. (8–20) I-do → we-do — who is it for? Show the dogs page (children), the uniform page (parents), the gaming page (adults). The words and pictures give away the audience. Pupils MATCH each page to its audience (matching grid).
3. (20–35) The features of a web page. Logo (top corner), header (title band), layouts (boxes), media. Show Google Sites and the four header types and six layouts. Support pupils choose where the logo goes / what the header is; Core name two features and a media type.
4. (35–50) You-do — plan your page on paper. Website name, logo, header, layouts, text and picture boxes. Leave the example on the board. Challenge: match the header/pictures to a young audience. Screenshot the design. TA: prompt with the help sheet; do not draw it for them.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the three 'I can…'; each pupil names one web page feature.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Copyright or copyWRONG?
lessons.objectives:
I can say why I should use copyright-free images
I can find copyright-free images
I can describe what is meant by 'fair use'
I know how to use technology respectfully and responsibly
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Words on the board: copyright · fair use · Creative Commons · copyright-free · credit. TA: the starter and activity worksheets open; the school-approved image search ready. Read the four 'I can…' aloud. Recap: last lesson we planned our page; now we find images for it.
1. (0–8) Starter — where do pictures come from? Build a class list (keep it for the plenary). The person who MADE a picture owns it. Likely belief: 'everything online is free' → we will see it is not. Support pupils choose who owns a picture.
2. (8–20) I-do → we-do — copyright and fair use. Copyright = the maker owns it, ask permission. Fair use = sometimes OK to use a small part, with credit. Be respectful — credit the people whose work you use. Ask 'how would you feel if someone stole your work?'
3. (20–32) Creative Commons. Show the CC licences chart. Creative Commons / copyright-free images are shared for others to use, often if you credit the maker. Model choosing 'Creative Commons' in the search. Pupils find and SAVE a copyright-free image; screenshot it. Follow the school's online-safety steps if anything unsuitable appears.
4. (32–48) You-do — copyright or copyWRONG? Pupils SORT scenarios into OK / Not OK and debate the ethics. Fix-words: 'even if it looks the same, still CHECK the licence.' Challenge covers the 'looks the same' case.
5. (48–55) Plenary — look back at the starter list: which places are actually safe? Self-tick the four 'I can…'.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: How does it look?
lessons.objectives:
I can add content to my own web page
I can preview what my web page looks like
I can evaluate what my web page looks like on different devices and suggest/make edits
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Words on the board: home page · preview · evaluate · device · Google Sites. TA: devices ready with Google Sites; the paper plans from previous lessons; the starter and activity worksheets open. Read the three 'I can…' aloud. Recap: last lesson we found copyright-free images; today we build the page.
1. (0–8) Starter — the steps to build. Show the set-up videos. Pupils ORDER the steps (open Google Sites → name the site → header → layout → content). Support pupils choose what the home page is / which tool we use.
2. (8–28) You-do — build your page. Use the paper plan; add the website name, logo, header, text and pictures. A long calm build block. TA: prompt with the layout boxes; do not build it for them. Early finishers: exploratory task (header colour, image carousel, map).
3. (28–40) Preview on devices. People use phones, tablets and computers; screens are different sizes. Use Preview to see the page on each. Likely belief: 'it looks the same everywhere' → fix-words 'different SCREEN SIZES.' Thumbs up/down on the phone view together.
4. (40–50) Evaluate and edit. Pupils tick each device (good / needs editing) and suggest one edit. Challenge: fix text missing off the phone view (e.g. smaller header). Screenshot the page or a preview.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the three 'I can…'; each pupil says why we preview on different devices.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Follow the breadcrumbs
lessons.objectives:
I can explain what a navigation path is
I can describe why navigation paths are useful
I can make multiple web pages and link them using hyperlinks
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Words on the board: breadcrumb trail · navigation · hyperlink · subpage. TA: devices with Google Sites; the starter and activity worksheets open. Read the three 'I can…' aloud. Recap: last lesson we built a home page; today we add more pages and link them.
1. (0–8) Starter — breadcrumbs. In Hansel and Gretel, breadcrumbs show the way back; on a website, a breadcrumb trail / navigation path shows where you have been. Support pupils choose what a navigation path is; Core explains the story link.
2. (8–20) I-do → we-do — where will you go? Show the zoo website. Talk through a route live (home → Animals → cheetah → back → arctic fox). Pupils ORDER a navigation path. Fix-words: 'go BACK to the page, then click the next link.'
3. (20–32) Plan your website. A site map has the home page at the top and subpages branching off. Pupils list the subpages they will link. Challenge: why plan the path first? TA: prompt one branch at a time.
4. (32–50) You-do — subpages and hyperlinks. A subpage is a page linked from another; a hyperlink is what you click to move between pages. Show the videos; pupils add subpages and hyperlink them. Likely error: a link that goes nowhere → check each works. Screenshot the linked pages.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the three 'I can…'; each pupil says what a navigation path is.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Think before you link!
lessons.objectives:
I can explain the implication of linking to content owned by others
I can create hyperlinks to link to other people's work
I can evaluate the user experience of a website
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Words on the board: hyperlink · external link · implication · evaluate · embed. TA: devices with Google Sites; the example website ready; the starter and activity worksheets open. Read the three 'I can…' aloud. End-of-unit lesson — recap the whole journey at the end.
1. (0–8) Starter — is it OK to link? A link to someone else's site is an external link; it is OK to link, but think first. Link back to copyright. Support pupils choose what an external link is.
2. (8–22) I-do → we-do — implications. The page might be deleted (link breaks); the content might change; the site might not be secure or trustworthy; give credit; open links in a new tab so users can get back. Pupils MULTI-SELECT what to think about. Fix-words: 'open in a NEW tab so the user can get BACK.' Brief online-safety chat: only sensible sites; report concerns.
3. (22–34) You-do — add an external link. Pupils search for a sensible, trustworthy site and link to it with credit. Early finishers: embedding content (show the video). Avoid sites with adverts / suggested content.
4. (34–50) Evaluate the user experience. Evaluate = judge how good it is; user experience = what it is like to use. Model the evaluation on the example site (spelling error, broken link). Agree the 1–5 scale (5 = great). Pupils RATE it (scale) then evaluate their own site. Challenge: why do a spelling mistake and a broken link matter? Screenshot the site + link.
5. (50–55) End of unit — recap: good websites → web designer → copyright → look on devices → navigation paths → linking safely. Celebrate the finished websites. Self-tick the three 'I can…'.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.
